speaker: interviewer
text: This is March 24th, 1995. We're interviewing Rose Leibowitz. And this is tape three. Rose, you were telling us about the period in hiding.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when the ghetto-- I think we were, me, my husband, and very few more people, were one of the last people to leave Meserich. There was a group that ran away differently. Every group, you know, [? you'd get together a group ?] and you'd go different places. When we ran away from the lumber yard and we went into the woods, and the people started chasing us, and we dispersed, I was holding on to my husband. And we went to Jelnica. It's where-- I was there, and it was daylight, still, and when the corn was fairly high already, because it was July. And we went, we were hiding in the corn. All of a sudden, I heard talking. And we were you know, hiding, and were afraid. We didn't know who was talking. And then we realized that they speak Jewish. So we met another couple, [NON-ENGLISH], the relations to the man that came back from the United States. And the two of them were hiding there. And they were parents of close friends of ours-- the same-- they had a son our age. And we were fairly close with them. And then the four of us were together, for awhile. And the men had a bunker in the woods. And they let us use that bunker. And we used it for about six weeks. We used to pay him for the food and for different things that he did for us. And we stayed there for about six weeks. At that time, the war-- war started in Italy-- Italy, I think-- in 1943, in August. It was about to be liberated, you know. And he thought that the war was going to be over. But the war was dragging on, and he said that he's afraid, and we have to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: So me and my husband decided we're going to go back to where we were working, where I was working, by the river, by the [NON-ENGLISH], because we knew-- we talked to the people. And they said, if we're going to be of some kind of-- in some kind of trouble or something, maybe we should have a place where to meet. So we figured, we're going to go back there. And maybe we're going to see somebody over there, on the other side of Meserich. So we walked back there. It was about 10, 12 kilometers. And we walked back there. And it was a very dark night. And as we were walking, my husband missed the place where they told us to go. And it was very-- a person could sink into it. And I was screaming and yelling, let's go and get it over with, because we can't get out. We can't do it anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at that particular time, I guess God wanted us to still be around. My husband found a place that was hard. And he was able to cross it. And he dragged me across. And we went to that place, where I used to work by the [NON-ENGLISH]. We met up over there with six other people. I just know two people, they came-- two boys. They came from the same town, like my husband. And the other four, I didn't-- I didn't know. I just met them at that time. They came from a small town around Meserich. It was called [NON-ENGLISH]. And all four of them-- we were there about six weeks. There was so much rain, and we didn't have nothing. We just slept under the skies. And my husband and the other men used to go back in the evening, to get us dry clothes. They used to cross the river and go into the ghetto, and get some things. In the ghetto, they met another two men-- one man that just came back from Treblinka, and another [? learned ?] man-- [NON-ENGLISH], they called him. And they brought those two men to us. And we were sitting-- we used to go out in the evening. We used to go out on the field and dig up potatoes and carrots, and whatever was growing. And we used to cook in water. We used to make a little fire, and at one particular place. And we used to cook our-- whatever we had a chance, we used to cook. I guess the people realized that we were there. And one day, that was just before Sukkot. That [? learned ?] man said, today is Sukkot, and we got to build a sukkah. And he went up to look, to find a place where to build a sukkah, or where probably-- if we would be normal people, we would build a sukkah. Just then, an idea about a sukkah. And we went up there. He went first, and the other men. And I followed him. One of the girls from [NON-ENGLISH], Malka her name was, was sitting in that spot, where we were cooking and peeling potatoes. And she was killed, instantly. And her brother was running towards her. So he caught a bullet in his shoulder. And we all dispersed, again, and ran away. At night we came back, again, to the same place, to see who was alive and who wasn't. Just that girl was dead. We didn't even have a shovel to bury her. Her brother and the other three people and one of the boys from my husband's hometown, [NON-ENGLISH], went with them to [NON-ENGLISH], because that's where they came, originally, from. And myself, my husband, and another boy from my husband's town of [NON-ENGLISH], and that man-- no relations to me, but his name was Goldstein, also-- he came from [NON-ENGLISH] in Poland. And he just escaped from Majdanek. He wrapped himself in clothing, which they were sending on the train to Germany. And as he realized he's close to Meserich, because Majdanek wasn't so far away from Meserich, so he knew, approximately, where it was. So he jumped from the train, and he came back to the ghetto. And the man brought a lot of money with him. And thanks to him, and the money that he had, we were able to-- to have things, and to eat, and to-- and the man was, you know, holding us. Because, I don't know if he, without money, if he would do something for us. But we came back. We went-- me, my husband, and that man-- and they had a boy. We went all the way back to Jelnica, another 10, 12 kilometers. And that was already after Sukkot. That was already towards the end of October. And we went back to Jelnica. And we came back to the man. And we told him, we're going to pay him for food. And we would like to build ourselves a place in the woods-- a bunker-- so we could spend the winter here.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he said fine, I'll-- I'll let you build one. But you got to give me so much, and so much. So we gave him the amount that he asked. And we built a bunker. And by the time we built the bunker, the [NON-ENGLISH] came back to us. And another girl-- her name was Dora-- she came back. So there were-- so there were seven of us. And we built a bunker. And we were in the bunker. And the man was supplying us with food. And every time there was good news on the front with the war, he was very happy and very good. Then, if something was bad, it started out again. You have to-- you have to leave. You have to leave. You have to leave. Well, he always changed his mind with a few zlotys.

speaker: interviewee
text: After a while, another girl came back to us. She found us, and she came. So we were eight. She was from [NON-ENGLISH]. Her name was Stetchen. And the eight of us stayed there winter. Nobody was allowed to go to that man. We used to go on [? stilts, ?] because if there was snow, you couldn't make footprints. And this place was not far from the [? horse ?] cemetery, where they buried the-- the hundred or so Jews that they killed. So the men used to go and say Kaddish there after midnight. When they first went, it was horrible, because they-- they used to come home. And they used to say that the graves-- the people are still alive, because they think it's shaking, that the earth is shaking. But after a while, it quiet down. And they used to go for quite awhile, as long as they could, they used to go and say Kaddish there. And then, we work with the-- with the Polish people all through the winter. It was fine. You know, when you turned, one person turned, everybody had to turn. When one person sat up, everybody had to, you know, had to join in. Whatever anybody did, we all had to do the same thing. That's how much space we had. Afterwards, in-- during the evening, towards the evening, we used to go. We used to gather food. We used to [? trap ?] [? stuff. ?] But mainly, I used to go and get the food, because nobody else was allowed to go with him. He didn't want nobody. But I used to go. I was the only one to go. After-- after quite a while, and when the news was good, it was very good and generous to us. There was already January, February, and March. Then it started out, the partisans-- the Russians dropped partisans. And when the [INAUDIBLE] would try to get a hold, and go connect with the partisans. But we never could come together with them. Whenever we-- we heard the rumor that they're here. And if we went there, [BACKROUND NOISE] they were gone, already. We always were a little too late. So we all stuck together. And at that particular time, they started being that the rails went through there. Every night, there was another explosion. The partisans used to dynamite the railroad tracks. So there were other explosions, and things. And when spring came, the man insisted, we have to go away. They were afraid. They were afraid the Germans were-- there was a lot of Germans nearby. And they were afraid they're going to kill them. They're going to burn them up in the houses. At that particular time, I don't remember how it was. I was walking or something, and I came across-- he was-- he was the mayor of the little village. In Europe, they used to call them a [NON-ENGLISH]. And he said--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, burgermeister.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, burgermeister is German. And he used to say-- he knew me, because we were renting from him quite a lot of summers. We were there, renting the place by him. So when he saw me, naturally he said-- first thing the Polish people said, when they saw a Jew-- are you still alive? But he was nice. He used to take food and even-- he was going into the field. He used to leave me packages of food. It was soft-boiled eggs, baked potatoes, whatever. Whatever. You know, he used to wrap it in a little package. And he used to leave it right there. I could never come to his house. I could never come communicate with him, or nothing. But he used to leave it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, there was another man that used to help us. But particularly, there was one very poor Polish farmer. They had a son that he went to Germany-- was a, what do you call it? A prisoner of war. And when he came back on furlough from Germany, they'd let him go back home. He took a Jewish girl with him, to Germany, as a Polish-- Polish girl. I don't know how they made her a Polish passport. And he took her with him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was-- there was nothing with him. He just took her out of his good heart. They weren't married, nothing. And she's in Australia, now. And he had a brother and two sisters, and an old mother. When we-- when spring started to come, and they said that we have to leave, we started to say when the corn is going to go-- grow higher, and we'll be able to go away, to hide in the corn. We're going to leave. We're not going to make any trouble for nobody. We're going to leave. But the corn has to grow a little higher. But they were-- I guess a lot of people in that thing knew about us, but they just didn't pay no attention. They didn't give us-- give us away to the Germans. Because like nothing, they could have told on us. But they didn't. And they were very nice. And we were there a year. We were there until we were liberated, because we couldn't have an occasion to go away. Everything was happening-- anything that's happening was happening right there. There's was a lot of Jewish boys killed there, in the [? same ?] Jelnica. One of our friends came to us, and we told him to stick with us, not to go away by himself. And he didn't listen. And he went away, and he got killed. The following night, he was dead. Because there were-- there were the [NON-ENGLISH] working at that time-- very, very high. And they were just looking for Jews to kill. That's all. And we-- we were liberated in July. We were there a little over a-- a little over a-- a week more than a year, we were there, in hiding.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Rose, these are mainly Polish, gentile farmers that were helping you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long would you say you were in hiding? And do you remember the names of these people that helped you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. There was one Witkowski, one Kavenski, one Podgani, and especially Noresto Renkus. They were marvelous. They never asked for money. They never asked for nothing. I remember the last three weeks when we were in hiding, we had to go-- had to separate from the eight. So me, my husband, and one of the girls-- Dora-- we, the three of us went away. And the boy, their son, the younger son, took us across the river. And that's where the Polish children were hiding, at that time. Because the Germans were catching the Polish young people, to-- to work in Germany. So that's where he was hiding. And he took us there to his hiding place. And he smiled at that old woman. She could hardly walk. She used to take, in the evening, her basket with pretending that she's going to the river to wash clothes. She put food for us in there. And the boy was standing there, hiding in the-- by the river. There was a lot of [NON-ENGLISH]. I don't even know what [NON-ENGLISH] in English. They were-- it wasn't woods, but there were plants, and things different, growing. So he was hiding there. And after it got dark, he used to swim across the river and bring us the food, and stay overnight with us, because he was hiding himself, too. And then in the morning he used to go back to work on the farm. That went on for three weeks. And the war was [? right ?] [? ended there. ?] We were in a place where the-- the earth was very soft, because otherwise, all this-- the bullets and the shrapnels, and the things that were falling there-- if they would, you know, tear apart. They would kill us. But everything sinked into the dirt.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was rain-- raining for three weeks. And my husband decided, he's gotta eat something. For one week, or more than one week, we didn't have nothing. They couldn't come to-- to bring us anything. So my husband said, he's not going to die of hunger, now. He doesn't care. He's going. There was instinct, now. So we went across the river. And we went to that Polack, to that Podgani. And the Germans were sitting on the veranda. And they didn't know we were Jews, because we were dressed just like the shepherds-- barefoot.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [INAUDIBLE] and we ran in. And we knew where we were going. And we went right away, downstairs to him, through the cellar. And we took whatever we want. And we went back. And they didn't ask us nothing. The reason they didn't ask is because they were already, at that time, plenty big [INAUDIBLE], themselves, the Germans. And two days later, the war-- the war-- we were liberated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you call these people that helped you what's known as righteous gentiles?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think they were very nice, yeah. I think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said one man-- could you tell us his name? He hid you for money, but then the others just hid you out of the goodness of their hearts?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. The other, they were-- one man was just plain businessman. And I'm sorry he lost everything when the Russians came. When the Germans left, they took the horses. When the Russians came in, they took the rest of the, whatever he had. Because the front was there. So I'm sorry he lost everything. But the was-- I don't think there was a lot of people that did what he did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rose, how long would you say you were in hiding, and what kind of place were you living in? I mean, what was it built out of?

speaker: interviewee
text: What was build of? They took a few two-by-fours and pushed up-- they digged-- they digged in the woods, they digged a hole. And they took some two-by-fours and things, to push it up, you know, so the dirt wouldn't fall in. They just made like a bunker. And the bunker was made from woods. And on the top of the bunker, they planted trees. And we had a little opening, just as big as this seat of the chair, where we went out, just at night. All day, we were sitting, our [INAUDIBLE] our legs crisscrossed, or laying on the back, or laying on the side. And when anybody laid on their back, the rest had to lay on their back. And if somebody turned on their side, everybody had to turn.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very cramped.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they-- very cramped, very cramped. And everything was done all day in the-- in the bunker. We were there-- first, we were there six weeks, but that was another bunker. That was the bunker that the men had built, before. But then, when we left and we came, and there was more of us, we had to build a larger bunker. So we built a larger bunker. And we built a bunker for seven. And then they eighth-- the eighth girl came, so it became really cramped. And we were there from when we came back, after Sukkot, like October, we were there until the last three weeks before we were liberated, when we ran away. We were there like October, November, December, January, February, March, April, May, and June.

speaker: interviewer
text: So these woods you were hiding-- these were-- would you say these were the outskirts of Meserich, in the woods and the meadows?

speaker: interviewee
text: Five kilometers away from Meserich.

speaker: interviewer
text: Meserich.

speaker: interviewee
text: Five kilometers away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So all of these happenings-- both where you grew up, and in the war period when they made it a ghetto, and all of this hiding-- basically all happened around Meserich and the outlying area?

speaker: interviewee
text: I spent all my life in the war years, and after the war years-- everything was around by [INAUDIBLE] Meserich. Everything I did was around Meserich-- five kilometers this way, eight kilometers the other way, but always around-- because I wasn't familiar with anything else. And I knew if I go anyplace else, I'm going to get killed, because I didn't know where to go. And I didn't know any people. But here, at least I knew-- I knew somebody, and that somebody knew me. That's-- that's where I was. That's where we all was. And when we were liberated, we went back to Meserich. And in Meserich, we found people. There was, I think, about 29 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, could we just go back for a second? Could you tell us what happened with your family, when you found out what happened with your family-- your father, your mother, your brother, and your relatives during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, like I said, my mother died a natural death, before the war. My father, I don't know. My mother had three sisters. They all-- my-- my aunt Faigi was killed with the last people, the last hundred people. And I guess my cousin Chaya was killed at that time, too. And so was my mother's only brother. He was killed there, too, because he was working by the clothing [INAUDIBLE]. And all the people that were working by the clothing sorting, they were killed there, by the [? horse ?] cemetery. And that's where they were killed. But my mother's oldest sister and my mother's youngest sister went away right away in 1942 with the first action. They went away at that time. And so did my husband's mother, and his younger brother and sister. They went away with the first action. And my-- my husband's two aunts and two uncles, with their families, they completely disappeared. Later, one-- one uncle was there-- was still alive, until 1943-- May of '43. His father, the two-- two brothers, they were younger than my husband. And one of his uncles. They were alive until 1943, where my brother said he saw in Majdanek. Because in May, my brother, my cousin, and my uncle were taken out from their hiding place, and taken to Majdanek. And so was my husband's father, and two brothers, and two uncles. They were taken to Majdanek in 1943. But they were taken out from different bunkers. One was taken out from the bunker from the ghetto. And the other one were taken out from the bunker where we worked in the [GERMAN]. That's the place where the Germans kept the people that they absolutely needed to work, and they were good workers. And still, they realize that they're not good workers, and they took them all away. That was to Majdanek in 1943-- May-- the 2nd of May. And then, my brother was in Majdanek. And then they took a bunch of people to Auschwitz. So him, and my cousin, and my cousin's father-- my uncle-- that did so much for myself and my brother, during the war, they were taken to Auschwitz. My uncle got killed in Auschwitz. My brother and my cousin were lucky to not to perish. They lived through the war. My brother escaped when they evacuated Auschwitz. My brother escaped.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jacob.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he came back to Meserich. My cousin came back to Meserich, after the war in May, '45. She came back to Meserich. Just before, maybe three, four weeks before I left Meserich, she came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us what happened to Meserich, after the ghetto was disbanded? Was it still standing, or was it leveled, or what happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, people started to just-- to take apart houses. They just plain took-- took apart everything-- especially the ghetto places. They were taking everything apart, brick by brick, wood by wood, they used to take out everything. As a matter of fact, when I came back after the first action in 1942, when I came back to my place, it was almost empty. They took out all the personal things. I didn't have nothing what to wear. They just robbed everything. They just went in and robbed it.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in the marketplace with the Germans. And the people, the Poles, mostly Polacks, they helped themselves to all the Jewish [NON-ENGLISH]. You know what [NON-ENGLISH] is? For all their fortunes. For all their things that they have, that they possess. Was your house still standing, or was that leveled?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was standing until 1943. It was still-- not everything. Part of the walls-- part of the walls. The reason that they-- I say part of the walls, because they keep taking apart walls, because in a lot of the places, they find double walls. And there was things hidden in there. So that's why they used to take it apart.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. Could you tell us the correct pronunciation of the street you were born on, and the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I was born [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And the province?

speaker: interviewee
text: Meserich, you mean in Polish, I should tell you? It used to be called Miedzyrzec Podlaski [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And how would you say that in English? Meserich?

speaker: interviewee
text: Meserich. Meserich. The district was [INAUDIBLE] and state-- what do you call-- Lublin. State, like the state of Illinois, over there it was [INAUDIBLE] Lublin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It was-- Lublin was a Jewish city, too-- a big Jewish city. And then Majdanek, all the people that-- I-- from people that I hear, it is that my husband lost his family in Majdanek just before the Russians liberated it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: People-- people [INAUDIBLE], the people that lived through came back, and was telling of a different people.
